item.title: Algebraic Foundations of Learning Systems
item.description:
Algebraic Foundations of Learning Systems

The algebraic learning calculus proposes that learning — whether by a human apprentice, a neural network, or an agent system accumulating instincts — has algebraic structure that goes beyond scalar confidence scores. This text surveys the published mathematics that either supports or constrains that claim.

The question is specific: where do established mathematical frameworks connect to the idea that confidence is closure convergence, that relevance is bilattice composition, and that concept formation is a lattice operation? Where are these connections grounded in published work, and where are they speculative?

item.title: Historical Development of the Function Concept
item.description:
Dedekind’s Abbildung

Dedekind introduced the notion of an Abbildung (mapping or image-making) in “Was sind und was sollen die Zahlen?” (1888). An Abbildung is a law ϕ\phi that assigns to each element ss of a set SS a definite element ϕ(s)\phi(s) of a set S′S'. The defining conditions are totality (every ss has an image) and functionality (the image is uniquely determined). Dedekind observed that the identity mapping and the composition of mappings are again mappings, and that mappings are the primary tool for establishing correspondences between mathematical structures. The Dedekind Abbildung is the direct ancestor of the category-theoretic morphism.

Reflexive Calibration Theory: Consistency as Residuation

Abstract

This paper develops a categorical formulation of calibration and measurement consistency as a reflexive process. Calibration is treated not as an external correction between instruments, but as the restoration of residuated sufficiency between recognitions within a common relational field. We formalize this in the algebra of Proposing a Reflexive Existence (v0.1), where operators \(Together\), \(Induces\), and \(Includes\) define the internal law \[ Includes(Together(a,b),c) \iff Includes(a,Induces(b,c)). \] Measurement disagreement is represented as the failure of this equivalence. The resulting reflexive calibration index \( g = 1 - V(Together(a,b))/V(Either(a,b)) \) generalizes standard overlap-based consistency measures, unifying calibration, uncertainty propagation, and statistical coherence under a single residuated framework.[cite:@ProposingReflexiveExistence]

Reflexive Calibration Theory: Consistency as Residuation

Abstract

This paper develops a categorical formulation of calibration and measurement consistency as a reflexive process. Calibration is treated not as an external correction between instruments, but as the restoration of residuated sufficiency between recognitions within a common relational field. We formalize this in the algebra of Proposing a Reflexive Existence (v0.1), where operators \(Together\), \(Induces\), and \(Includes\) define the internal law \[ Includes(Together(a,b),c) \iff Includes(a,Induces(b,c)). \] Measurement disagreement is represented as the failure of this equivalence. The resulting reflexive calibration index \( g = 1 - V(Together(a,b))/V(Either(a,b)) \) generalizes standard overlap-based consistency measures, unifying calibration, uncertainty propagation, and statistical coherence under a single residuated framework.[cite:@ProposingReflexiveExistence]
